does: SGuard-v1 is a lightweight dual-component safety system for large language models consisting of ContentFilter and JailbreakFilter modules built on a 2B-parameter Granite model supporting 12 languages. ContentFilter detects harmful content in prompts and responses using the MLCommons hazard taxonomy as its classification framework. JailbreakFilter identifies adversarial prompts through curriculum learning over integrated datasets covering 60 major attack types, specifically designed to reduce false-positive unsafe classifications. The system was trained on approximately 1.4 million curated and synthesized instances through instruction tuning, with data strategically distributed between components based on their safety assessment functions.
connects: This work extends the growing literature on safety mechanisms for LLMs by building on prior research in adversarial prompt detection and content filtering. It incorporates findings from studies on jailbreaking attacks and adversarial prompting while leveraging established safety taxonomies like MLCommons standards that consolidate knowledge about AI hazard categorization. The dual-component architecture responds to the recognized need for both general content moderation and specialized detection of emerging attack patterns. SGuard-v1 advances beyond single-stage filtering by adopting curriculum learning strategies previously successful in adversarial robustness work, and by synthesizing diverse attack datasets to achieve broader coverage of threat vectors identified in recent jailbreak research.
enables: SGuard-v1 enables practical deployment of safety guardrails at scale across multilingual conversational AI systems by providing a lightweight, resource-efficient alternative to larger safety models. The comprehensive coverage of 60 attack types and multilingual support creates a foundation for evaluating LLM safety across diverse linguistic and cultural contexts. This work points toward future research on curriculum-based safety training, systematic characterization of emerging jailbreak techniques, and methods for reducing false-unsafe rates without compromising security. The system could enable broader safety benchmarking efforts and facilitate integration of safety layers into production LLM pipelines, while also supporting investigation of how attack coverage generalizes to novel adversarial prompting strategies.
